question: Will the average monthly Ethereum gas price hit 15 Gwei before 2027?
description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if the average monthly Ethereum gas price is equal to or greater than the value specified in the title by December 31, 2026 ET. Otherwise, the market will resolve to “No”.

The resolution source for this market is the dashboard available at https://dune.com/nibty/eth-gas-prices, using the “mean_gas” value displayed in the “Gas Prices Monthly Average” tab.

This market will resolve as soon as a finalized average monthly gas price shown on the dashboard is equal to or greater than the value specified in the title, or once the value for December 2026 is finalized and no earlier finalized monthly value has met or exceeded that threshold.

A monthly value will be considered finalized once the next monthly data point is published.

If the resolution source becomes permanently unavailable, the market will resolve based on other reliable crypto data sources.

question: Joe Burrow traded to the Jets?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if Joe Burrow of the Cincinnati Bengals is traded to the New York Jets before the start of the first 2026 NFL preseason game. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

If Burrow is cut, retires, or is not on an NFL roster as of the resolution time, this market will resolve to "No".

The resolution source for this market will be official information from the Cincinnati Bengals and the New York Jets, however a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

events.title: Joe Burrow traded to the Jets?
events.description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if Joe Burrow of the Cincinnati Bengals is traded to the New York Jets before the start of the first 2026 NFL preseason game. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

If Burrow is cut, retires, or is not on an NFL roster as of the resolution time, this market will resolve to "No".

The resolution source for this market will be official information from the Cincinnati Bengals and the New York Jets, however a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Joe Burrow's massive five-year, $275 million contract extension with the Bengals, signed in September 2023, anchors him as Cincinnati's franchise quarterback, making a trade highly improbable without catastrophic team collapse. Bengals GM Duke Tobin has repeatedly affirmed Burrow as the cornerstone of their perennial playoff contender, backed by recent injury recovery updates showing him on track for Week 1. The Jets, meanwhile, remain committed to Aaron Rodgers despite his 2023 Achilles tear, with no credible reports from beat writers or league sources fueling swap speculation—trader consensus reflects this stability, pricing "No" at 96.3%. Realistic wildcards include Burrow's injury recurrence prompting a Bengals rebuild or Jets' total Rodgers fallout, though both remain remote given current roster builds and cap realities.

question: Will Welsh Labour win the most seats in the 2026 Welsh Senedd election?
description:
Parliamentary elections are scheduled to be held in Wales on May 7, 2026.

This market will resolve according to the political party that wins the greatest number of seats in the Welsh Parliament (Senedd) in this election.

If voting in the Welsh parliamentary election does not occur by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to "Other".

In the event of a tie between multiple parties for the most seats won, this market will resolve in favor of the party or coalition which received a greater number of valid votes. In the event that results in a tie, this market will resolve in favor of the party or coalition whose listed abbreviation appears first in alphabetical order.

This market's resolution will be based solely on the number of seats won by the named party or coalition in the Welsh Parliament. 

This market will resolve based on the results of this election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the Welsh government, specifically the Senedd Commission of Wales  (https://senedd.wales/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket heavily favors Plaid Cymru at 73.5% implied probability to win the Welsh Parliament election, reflecting recent polls showing the party leading Welsh Labour by double digits amid the latter's leadership crisis following Vaughan Gething's July 2024 resignation over donation scandals. Reform UK trails at 26.5%, buoyed by its strong third-place finish in the July UK general election across Welsh constituencies and rising support on immigration and economic discontent. Welsh Labour's slim 0.7% odds stem from new leader Eluned Morgan's shaky start and stagnant approval ratings. Other parties linger below 1%, as proportional representation favors larger blocs. Upcoming local polls and budget votes could shift dynamics before the 2026 Senedd contest.

eventMetadata.context_description: Scottish National Party's commanding 95.9% implied probability in the Scotland Parliamentary Election winner market reflects consistent polling leads projecting them to secure the most Holyrood seats in 2026, driven by a fragmented opposition and their lock on pro-independence voters. Recent surveys, including Find Out Now (37% SNP vs. 27% Labour) and Survation (36% SNP), underscore SNP advantages under John Swinney amid Labour's post-UK election slump and Conservative declines. Reform UK's 2.1% share tracks their rising Westminster vote share, but proportional representation limits breakthroughs. Realistic challenges include SNP governance scandals eroding trust, economic pressures favoring unionists, or opposition consolidation, though traders see low upset risk given historical base rates.

Parliamentary elections are scheduled to be held in Scotland on May 7, 2026.

This market will resolve according to the political party that wins the greatest number of seats in the Scottish Parliament  in this election.

If voting in the Scottish parliamentary election does not occur by July 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to "Other".

In the event of a tie between multiple parties for the most seats won, this market will resolve in favor of the party or coalition which received a greater number of valid votes. In the event that results in a tie, this market will resolve in favor of the party or coalition whose listed abbreviation appears first in alphabetical order.

This market's resolution will be based solely on the number of seats won by the named party or coalition in the Welsh Parliament. 

This market will resolve based on the results of this election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the Welsh government, specifically the the Electoral Commission of Scotland (https://www.electoralcommission.org.uk/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Scottish National Party's commanding 95.9% implied probability in the Scotland Parliamentary Election winner market reflects consistent polling leads projecting them to secure the most Holyrood seats in 2026, driven by a fragmented opposition and their lock on pro-independence voters. Recent surveys, including Find Out Now (37% SNP vs. 27% Labour) and Survation (36% SNP), underscore SNP advantages under John Swinney amid Labour's post-UK election slump and Conservative declines. Reform UK's 2.1% share tracks their rising Westminster vote share, but proportional representation limits breakthroughs. Realistic challenges include SNP governance scandals eroding trust, economic pressures favoring unionists, or opposition consolidation, though traders see low upset risk given historical base rates.

question: Will the number of Republican Senate members who retire in 2026 be exactly 5?
description:
The 2026 U.S. general elections for Congress are scheduled to be held on November 3, 2026.

This market will resolve according to the total number of Republican members of the U.S. Senate who are considered to be retiring or not seeking reelection in 2026.

For the purposes of this market, a member may be considered not to seek reelection if:
• They publicly announce they are retiring from Congress and will not run for reelection to their current seat
• They choose to run for a different elected office in 2026 instead of running for reelection to their current seat
• They do not seek their party’s nomination for reelection to their current seat, meaning they neither file for reelection nor participate in their party’s nominating process (such as a primary, convention, or caucus)

Only members whose current term is scheduled to end with the 2026 regular election for the Senate may qualify for this market.

This market will not resolve until after the relevant final 2026 candidate filing deadline has passed in all states, which is expected to occur in late August 2026.

The resolution source for this market will be a consensus of credible reporting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket prices a tight contest between 6 (36%) and 7 (37.5%) Republican senators not seeking re-election in the 2026 midterms, anchored by four confirmed Class 2 seat retirements: Mitch McConnell (Kentucky), Jerry Moran (Kansas), John Boozman (Arkansas, announced October 18), and Thom Tillis (North Carolina, October 30). This recent announcement cluster has elevated expectations from prior base rates, as aging incumbents face potential competitive primaries or general election risks in states like Iowa and Maine. The narrow spread reflects uncertainty over further exits from vulnerable holders like Joni Ernst or Susan Collins; additional retirements or re-election filings before early 2025 deadlines could decisively shift probabilities.

question: Will Russia capture all of Kupiansk by March 31?
description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if, according to the ISW map, Russia captures the entirety of Kupiansk by March 31, 2026, at 11:59 PM ET.

Kupiansk will be considered captured if the entirety of the municipality is shaded red on the ISW map (https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/36a7f6a6f5a9448496de641cf64bd375) by the resolution date. If the area is not shaded red by March 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, the market will resolve to “No”.

The territory of the municipality is shaded in light grey and is distinguished from bordering municipalities such as "Moskovka" with a darker grey line. If all area within the municipality is shaded in red, but the shading does not precisely match up with the border, such that there is a tiny amount of grey along the border of the city, this will still qualify for a "Yes" resolution.

For any change on the ISW map to qualify for this market’s resolution, the relevant shading indicating Russian control must persist through the next full ISW daily update cycle. If ISW skips a day, shading must persist until the next finalized ISW update is published, regardless of the date. Any continuous shading which reflects either “Assessed Russian Control”, “Assessed Russian Advance In Ukraine”, or “Assessed Russian Gains in the Past 24 Hours” will qualify. “Assessed Russian Infiltration Areas in Ukraine” will not qualify.


For the purpose of this market, areas separated from the main municipality through a darker grey line, such as in the southeast of Kupiansk (https://polymarket-upload.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/h-b99a52275d.png), will not count towards the municipality.

Once Russia captures Kupiansk, any subsequent loss of control will not be considered towards the resolution of this market.

Kupiansk Location: https://polymarket-upload.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/Kupiansk+location.jpeg
Location on Google maps: https://maps.app.goo.gl/ngCjTi39GSjbzaC46 

The primary resolution source for this market will be the ISW Ukraine map. If the ISW map is rendered unavailable, information from DeepStateMap (https://deepstatemap.live/) may be used. If information from both the ISW and DeepStateMap are rendered permanently unavailable, a consensus of credible reporting may be used.

Note: Any temporary glitches or errors in the map which will not be considered.

If Russia comes into control of this territory as a result of a negotiated settlement, this will qualify for a 'Yes' resolution, regardless of whether it is shaded red in the ISW map. An announcement of a negotiated settlement that gives Russia de jure control will not qualify. Actual control must be established.

eventMetadata.context_description: Russian forces have intensified offensives around Kupiansk, a key logistical hub in Ukraine's Kharkiv Oblast, capturing surrounding villages like Kruhliat and advancing to the town's outskirts in recent weeks, driving trader sentiment on Polymarket toward higher implied probabilities of full capture by year-end. Ukrainian troops maintain control of the city center despite heavy fighting and reported ammunition constraints, supported by entrenched defenses. Primary factors include Russia's manpower superiority and drone strikes versus Ukraine's fortified positions and incoming Western aid. Traders monitor winter weather impacts and potential frontline shifts, with no confirmed encirclement yet amid ongoing artillery duels.

question: Will SpaceX's public ticker be $SPAX?
description:
This market will resolve according to the public ticker symbol used by SpaceX in an IPO (Initial Public Offering) by December 31, 2027, 11:59 PM ET.

An official announcement from SpaceX that they will IPO under a specific ticker symbol will be sufficient to resolve this market.

If a ticker used by SpaceX in a qualifying IPO is a variant of a ticker symbol listed in this market group with additional letters to denote a specific class of shares, it will be considered to be that ticker (e.g. if SpaceX uses a ticker symbol of $MARS.A or $MARSA, this market will resolve to $MARS).

If SpaceX announces an IPO with multiple tickers that are not considered the same ticker under the previous rule (i.e. $MARS.A and $MARS.B would be considered the same ticker), this market will remain open until SpaceX’s first day of public trading and will resolve according to the ticker symbolizing the security class with the greatest market capitalization. Market capitalization is calculated as the number of shares outstanding multiplied by the closing share price on the first trading day. If this also results in a tie, this market will resolve according to the ticker symbol that comes first in alphabetical order.

If SpaceX IPOs with a non-listed ticker, or does not IPO or officially announce an IPO and ticker symbol by December 31, 2027, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to “Other.”

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from SpaceX and the primary exchange’s official listing page. In the event that the relevant figure is not displayed, another reliable source will be used.

Note: In the event of an interruption in the course of the normal trading session on SpaceX’s first day of trading (e.g., a circuit breaker or half-day), the market will use the official closing price of the abbreviated session. If no such official closing price is published, the market will use the closing price on the next trading day for which an official closing price is published, treating that as the first day of trading for purposes of this market.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment on SpaceX's hypothetical public ticker heavily favors $X at 45.5% implied probability, driven by Elon Musk's longstanding affinity for the symbol from his X.com PayPal predecessor and its alignment with the rebranded X platform, signaling brand synergy potential. $STAR trails at 13.8%, boosted by Musk's recent comments on a possible Starlink IPO in 2025 once cash flow stabilizes, decoupling it from core SpaceX operations amid Starship test successes. $SPAX (10.2%) reflects SPAC merger speculation, while $SPACE and $SX draw from intuitive branding shorthand. Absent any SEC filing or official IPO timeline, probabilities remain speculative, fluctuating with Musk's X posts and valuation spikes from $200 billion+ private rounds.

question: Will SpaceX's public ticker be $SX?
description:
This market will resolve according to the public ticker symbol used by SpaceX in an IPO (Initial Public Offering) by December 31, 2027, 11:59 PM ET.

An official announcement from SpaceX that they will IPO under a specific ticker symbol will be sufficient to resolve this market.

If a ticker used by SpaceX in a qualifying IPO is a variant of a ticker symbol listed in this market group with additional letters to denote a specific class of shares, it will be considered to be that ticker (e.g. if SpaceX uses a ticker symbol of $MARS.A or $MARSA, this market will resolve to $MARS).

If SpaceX announces an IPO with multiple tickers that are not considered the same ticker under the previous rule (i.e. $MARS.A and $MARS.B would be considered the same ticker), this market will remain open until SpaceX’s first day of public trading and will resolve according to the ticker symbolizing the security class with the greatest market capitalization. Market capitalization is calculated as the number of shares outstanding multiplied by the closing share price on the first trading day. If this also results in a tie, this market will resolve according to the ticker symbol that comes first in alphabetical order.

If SpaceX IPOs with a non-listed ticker, or does not IPO or officially announce an IPO and ticker symbol by December 31, 2027, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to “Other.”

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from SpaceX and the primary exchange’s official listing page. In the event that the relevant figure is not displayed, another reliable source will be used.

Note: In the event of an interruption in the course of the normal trading session on SpaceX’s first day of trading (e.g., a circuit breaker or half-day), the market will use the official closing price of the abbreviated session. question: Josh Allen x Hailee Steinfeld baby: Boy or Girl?
description:
On Dec 12, Hailee Steinfeld revealed she was pregnant (see: https://www.espn.com/nfl/story/_/id/47289078/buffalo-bills-josh-allen-hailee-steinfeld-pregnant).

This market will resolve to "Boy" if Josh Allen, Hailee Steinfeld, or one of their official representatives announces that they are having a boy.

This market will resolve to "Girl" if Josh Allen, Hailee Steinfeld, or one of their official representatives announces that they are having a girl.

If the biological sex of Hailee Steinfeld's child remains undisclosed by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to "50-50".

If Steinfeld gives birth to more than one child from this pregnancy, this market will resolve based on the biological sex of the children. If all children are of the same biological sex, the market will resolve as "Boy" if all are male, or "Girl" if all are female. If the children are of different sexes, the market will resolve to "50-50".

This market refers only to the current pregnancy. Any future pregnancies will not be considered for this market.

The resolution source for this market will be official information from Josh Allen and Hailee Steinfeld however a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican David Valadao holds a narrow edge in California's 22nd Congressional District primary based on recent polls, driving trader consensus toward his advancement alongside Democrat Rudy Salas in the state's top-two system. Recent fundraising reports show Valadao leading with over $1.5 million raised, bolstered by National Republican Congressional Committee support, while Salas benefits from Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee backing. Polling averages from Race to the WH and 538 indicate Valadao at 45-50% and Salas at 40-45%, with minor candidates splitting votes. The March 5 primary remains pivotal, as any late shifts in voter registration or turnout could alter the pairings for November's general election.

question: José Luís Rodríguez Zapatero arrested by March 31?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if former president of Spain José Luís Rodríguez Zapatero is arrested or detained by law enforcement by March 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to "No".

Temporary holding at a detention facility while awaiting a judge’s decision on whether to grant a detention warrant qualifies, so long as they are held by law enforcement in an official capacity during that time.

A qualifying arrest/detention includes:
* Being taken into physical custody by law enforcement authorities (including federal, state, local, military, or international law enforcement)
* Voluntarily surrendering to law enforcement authorities in response to an arrest warrant
* Being formally booked or processed following detention
* Being handcuffed by a state authority and taken to a police station
* Surrendering at a police station or courthouse with their attorney
* Being placed under house arrest or electronic monitoring

The following scenarios will NOT qualify as an arrest/detention
* An arrest warrant being issued but not executed
* Being questioned or interviewed without arrest
* Being named in an indictment without arrest

The primary resolution source for this market will be official information from the arresting government and law enforcement; however, a consensus of credible reporting may also be used.

Recent rumors, largely circulating on social media amid Venezuela election disputes where Zapatero served as a mediator, stem from unverified partisan claims by opposition figures but lack substantiation from primary sources like police statements or court filings. Zapatero's continued public engagements underscore the baseless nature of speculation. Realistic shifts could arise only from unforeseen indictments on legacy corruption probes or sudden diplomatic escalations, though historical precedents show such high-profile arrests require clear legal momentum absent here.

question: Will Civil Contract win the most seats in the 2026 Armenian National Assembly election?
description:
Parliamentary elections are scheduled to be held in Armenia on June 7, 2026.

This market will resolve according to the political party that wins the greatest number of seats in the Armenian National Assembly in this election.

If voting in the Armenian parliamentary election does not occur by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to "Other".

In the event of a tie between multiple parties for the most seats won, this market will resolve in favor of the party or coalition which received a greater number of valid votes. In the event that results in a tie, this market will resolve in favor of the party or coalition whose listed abbreviation appears first in alphabetical order.

This market's resolution will be based solely on the number of seats won by the named party or coalition in the Armenian National Assembly. 

This market will resolve based on the results of this election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the Armenian government, specifically the Armenian Central Election Commission (https://www.elections.am/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket strongly favors Civil Contract at 81% to win Armenia's parliamentary election, driven by the party's supermajority from the 2021 snap vote and consistent polling dominance under Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan. Incumbency advantages persist amid peace talks with Azerbaijan, despite public backlash over Nagorno-Karabakh losses and recent territorial concession protests. The Armenia Alliance holds 12.5% on opposition to Pashinyan's foreign policy, while fragmented rivals like Bright Armenia (4.4%) and Prosperous Armenia (0.4%) lag due to low visibility and internal divisions. Upcoming diplomatic milestones and 2026 campaign dynamics could shift odds, underscoring market sensitivity to geopolitical catalysts.

question: Will the Republican Party win the AZ-01 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the AZ-01 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Latest polling in Arizona's 1st Congressional District shows Democrat Amish Shah holding a slim 48-46 edge over incumbent Republican David Schweikert, per RMG Research, fueling the Polymarket's tight 51% Democratic vs. 46.5% Republican trader consensus. This suburban Phoenix toss-up remains neck-and-neck due to Schweikert's past ethics scrutiny offsetting his name recognition, Shah's fundraising surge on healthcare issues, and balanced partisan lean in a district Trump carried narrowly in 2020. National midterm headwinds and heavy ad spending keep it volatile; separation could emerge from the October 22 debate, early vote tallies, or high-profile endorsements shifting undecideds in this battleground House race.

National midterm headwinds and heavy ad spending keep it volatile; separation could emerge from the October 22 debate, early vote tallies, or high-profile endorsements shifting undecideds in this battleground House race.

question: Will the Republican Party win the AZ-02 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the AZ-02 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket favors the Republican Party at 64% implied probability in Arizona's 2nd Congressional District House race, driven primarily by incumbent Eli Crane's strong position in a district with R+5 partisan lean (Cook PVI). Recent polls, including a September RMG Research survey showing Crane leading Kirsten Engel 48%-42%, reinforce this edge amid Crane's fundraising advantage and consistent midterm base rates favoring incumbents. Engel's higher small-dollar donations provide Democratic momentum, but no major catalysts have shifted odds recently. Key upcoming events include early voting starting October 9 and a potential October debate, which could influence trader sentiment in this competitive but Republican-tilting contest.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket favors the Democratic Party at 61% implied probability to win Arizona's 6th Congressional District House seat, driven by recent polling averages showing Kirsten Engel leading incumbent Juan Ciscomani by 4-6 points in key surveys from Data for Progress and RMG Research. Engel's fundraising advantage—raising over $2.5 million compared to Ciscomani's $1.8 million—bolsters her ground game in this Tucson-area swing district, rated a Toss-up by Cook Political Report. Ciscomani's narrow 2022 victory and votes on border security draw mixed reviews amid national headwinds for Republicans, while early voting data indicates strong Democratic turnout. Upcoming debates could shift odds, but current pricing reflects Engel's momentum on issues like abortion rights and water policy.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic Party at 79.5% in California's 13th Congressional District House race, propelled by recent polls showing Adam Gray leading incumbent John Duarte by 8-12 points across multiple surveys, including those from Emerson College and The Grade. Gray, the 2022 Democratic nominee who fell short by under 2,000 votes, benefits from superior fundraising—over $3 million raised versus Duarte's $1.5 million—and gains among independent and Latino voters in this Republican-leaning Central Valley seat. Duarte faces headwinds from attack ads on his forgiven PPP loan and a national GOP ballot environment. Early voting data shows high Democratic turnout, solidifying the shift from toss-up to lean Democratic status per nonpartisan forecasters.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent polls showing Republican Jeff Hurd and Democrat Adam Frisch deadlocked around 45% each underpin the razor-thin trader consensus in Colorado's 3rd Congressional District House race, a swing seat spanning rural western Colorado where energy policy, immigration and water rights divide voters. Hurd benefits from GOP base consolidation post-Lauren Boebert's district switch, while Frisch leverages strong fundraising and past near-upset in 2022. High undecided rates and independent sway keep probabilities balanced amid national midterm headwinds. Separation could emerge from October debates, late absentee turnout data or shifts in generic ballot sentiment, with early voting already underway.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Yadira Caraveo's consistent polling edge over Republican challenger Gabe Evans drives trader consensus for a Democratic victory at 69.5% in Colorado's 8th Congressional District House race. Recent RMG Research (Oct. 23-25) shows Caraveo leading 48%-44%, with other surveys confirming a 4-6 point advantage amid her fundraising dominance—$4.2 million versus Evans' $1.6 million—and strength in suburban and Latino voter blocs. Early voting leans Democratic in this D+4 district, flipped narrowly in 2022, though national Republican House momentum tempers odds. No seismic shifts reported, with Election Day catalysts ahead.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the Connecticut 5th Congressional District House race heavily favors the Democratic incumbent at 75%, driven by Rep. Jahana Hayes' consistent polling edge over Republican George Logan in their 2022 rematch. A late October Emerson College survey showed Hayes leading 48%-42% among likely voters, reinforcing her fundraising superiority—$3.2 million cash-on-hand versus Logan's $1.1 million—and incumbency advantages in the R+3 district. National Republican momentum has provided Logan some lift to 15%, but Hayes' strong Black and suburban voter support sustains her position amid early voting underway. Traders price in low upset risk absent major shifts before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Florida's 23rd Congressional District, trader consensus tilts slightly toward Democratic incumbent Jared Moskowitz at 49% implied probability over Republican challenger Joe Budd's 48%, mirroring tight recent polls showing Moskowitz ahead by 2-4 points amid high uncertainty. The race stays competitive in this D+5 district due to strong GOP base turnout, Budd's law enforcement background appealing to moderates, and Moskowitz's fundraising edge offset by national headwinds for Democrats. Key separators include early voting trends starting this week, final debate performances, and spillover from Florida's abortion rights ballot measure boosting Democratic mobilization, with any polling shift or endorsement news potentially widening the gap before Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus strongly favors the Democratic Party at 87% implied probability to win Illinois's 17th Congressional District House seat, driven by incumbent Rep. Eric Sorensen's consistent double-digit polling leads over Republican nominee Joe McGraw. Recent polls, including a late October survey showing Sorensen ahead 53%-39%, underscore his strengthened position amid superior fundraising—over $1.4 million raised versus McGraw's $200,000—and high name recognition from his 2022 flip of the seat. The district's modest Democratic lean (D+3 Cook PVI) and suburban Rockford dynamics further support retention prospects, though national House control battles and final voter turnout could influence late shifts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent polls, including Emerson College's October survey showing Democratic incumbent Frank Mrvan at 47% to Republican Joel Sagastegui's 44%, underscore the razor-thin margin fueling trader sentiment in Indiana's 1st congressional district House race, with Democrats holding a slim 51% implied probability. The northwest Indiana district's working-class voters, where Mrvan won by 7 points in 2022 amid Biden's underperformance and Trump's narrow 2020 edge, keep dynamics competitive amid national Republican ballot momentum. Strong early voting turnout in Democratic Lake County bolsters Mrvan's position, but rural GOP gains could narrow it further; final absentee ballot data and Election Day mobilization stand as key catalysts for potential separation before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the Iowa's 1st Congressional District House race strongly favors Democrat Christina Bohannan at 73.5% implied probability over Republican incumbent Mariannette Miller-Meeks at 26%, driven primarily by recent polling leads. An Emerson College survey from early October showed Bohannan ahead 48%-44%, aligning with aggregates giving her a slight edge in this rematch of 2022's razor-thin Republican victory by six votes post-recount. Bohannan's superior fundraising—over $5 million raised versus Miller-Meeks' $3 million—and effective debate showings have shifted sentiment, amid the district's Republican-leaning partisan index. Early voting turnout and final pre-election polls remain key catalysts before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Iowa's 3rd Congressional District, trader consensus favors Democrats at 53.5% implied probability, driven by recent polls showing challenger Christina Bohannan leading incumbent Zach Nunn by 2-4 points in surveys from Emerson College and The Hill. This rematch follows Nunn's slim 2022 win, with Bohannan's fundraising edge—over $2 million raised versus Nunn's $1.2 million—bolstering her position amid Iowa's toss-up dynamics. Voter concerns on abortion restrictions, agriculture policy, and inflation have tightened the race in this R+3 district, holding Republicans at 31%. Upcoming October debates and early voting trends could further influence market sentiment ahead of November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the MI-07 House race favors the Republican nominee Tom Barrett at 55% implied probability over Democrat Curtis Greer at 42%, reflecting Barrett's polling edge in this open battleground district vacated by retiring Rep. Elissa Slotkin. Key drivers include Barrett's dominant August primary win, superior fundraising exceeding $2 million, and Trump endorsement amid a GOP-favorable national wave on economy and border issues. Early voting starts soon, with debates potentially swaying tight margins.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Michigan's 8th Congressional District, an open seat race after Rep. Elissa Slotkin's Senate bid, trader consensus favors Democrat Kristen McDonald Rivet at 65.5% implied probability over Republican Paul Junge at 38.5%, reflecting her edge in recent polls like Emerson's October survey (48%-43%) and internals showing wider leads. eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus favors the Democratic nominee at 56% implied probability to win Michigan's 10th Congressional District House seat, reflecting recent polls showing a narrow Democratic edge over Republican incumbent Lisa McClain. The district, with a Republican-leaning partisan voter index of R+2, remains highly competitive amid national swing-state dynamics in Michigan. Key drivers include a mid-September Emerson College poll indicating Democrat Carl leading 48%-44%, bolstered by strong Democratic fundraising and turnout efforts on issues like abortion rights following Michigan's 2022 ballot victory. McClain's incumbency provides a base, but trader sentiment has shifted post-polling amid early voting underway, with final pre-election surveys expected to influence odds before November 5.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus assigns an 83% implied probability to a Democratic victory in Nebraska's 2nd Congressional District House race, driven by consistent polling leads for challenger Tony Vargas over incumbent Republican Don Bacon. eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket heavily favors the Democratic Party at 87% implied probability to win Nevada's 1st Congressional District House seat, reflecting the district's consistent Democratic lean (Cook PVI D+6) and incumbent Rep. Dina Titus's entrenched position since 2012. Recent polls, including a September RMG Research survey showing Titus leading Republican challenger Mark Robertson 54%-32%, reinforce this edge amid strong urban voter turnout in Las Vegas. No major scandals or shifts have emerged, with Titus's fundraising advantage ($1.2M cash-on-hand vs. Robertson's $150K) bolstering her frontrunner status. Early voting begins October 21, but historical base rates suggest minimal upset risk in this safely blue seat.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket heavily favors the Democratic incumbent Susie Lee at 82.5% implied probability to win Nevada's 3rd Congressional District, driven by her consistent double-digit leads in recent polls, including an Emerson College survey from late October showing her ahead 52%-41%. Lee's fundraising dominance—over $3 million cash-on-hand versus challenger Ed Montenegro's under $500,000—and strong performance in the Las Vegas suburbs anchor this sentiment. Montenegro, a Republican neurosurgeon, trails amid Nevada's competitive but Democratic-leaning environment in Clark County. Early voting underway could shift dynamics, but no major catalysts have narrowed the gap recently, reflecting traders' assessment of Lee's incumbency edge and district fundamentals.

eventMetadata.context_description: In New Jersey's 5th congressional district House race, trader consensus tilts toward the Democratic nominee at 51.5% implied probability, driven by incumbent Josh Gottheimer's established moderate profile, fundraising dominance exceeding $10 million, and consistent double-digit leads in recent polls like the September Emerson College survey showing him ahead 48-37% over Republican challenger Mary Jo Murphy. The district's suburban swing voter base, which favored Biden by 4 points in 2020, bolsters this edge, though Republicans hold at 23.5% amid Murphy's primary win and GOP efforts to capitalize on inflation concerns. Recent developments include Gottheimer's endorsement from the bipartisan Problem Solvers Caucus and Murphy's uptick in outside spending, but no major shifts have closed the gap ahead of November balloting.

eventMetadata.context_description: In New Jersey's 7th Congressional District, trader consensus tilts slightly toward Democrats at 46% over Republicans at 38.5%, capturing a dead-heat race between incumbent Tom Kean (R) and Democratic challenger Allyson Muise amid recent polls showing ties within margins of error. Kean's narrow 2022 flip of the Republican-leaning suburban seat (Cook PVI R+4) is offset by Democratic advantages in fundraising—over $3 million cash-on-hand—and national party infrastructure targeting vulnerable GOP freshmen. Local factors like commuter rail funding and property taxes maintain equilibrium, with both sides securing key endorsements from unions and business groups. Upcoming early voting trends, a late October debate, or shifts in presidential coattails could tip probabilities decisively.

eventMetadata.context_description: In New Jersey's 9th Congressional District, a longtime Democratic stronghold with a D+12 partisan lean, trader consensus favors the Democratic Party at 78% implied probability, driven by nominee Shavonda Sumter's commanding lead over Republican Billy Prempeh. Rep. Bill Pascrell's death on October 21 prompted a swift Democratic nomination of Sumter, a veteran assemblywoman with strong local endorsements from Gov. Phil Murphy and Sen. Cory Booker. A late October Data for Progress poll shows Sumter ahead 55%-23%, aligning with the district's history of lopsided Democratic wins—Pascrell took 70% in 2022. Absent major shifts before the November 5 election, markets reflect low upset risk amid undecided voters.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket heavily favors a Democratic win in the NY-03 special election at 79%, with Republicans at 39%, driven by consistent polling leads for former Rep. Tom Suozzi over Republican Mazi Pilip. The district's moderate Democratic lean, combined with lingering fallout from George Santos' expulsion, bolsters Suozzi's position as a familiar centrist with prior experience holding the seat. Recent Emerson College polling (February 8) shows Suozzi ahead 57-37%, up from earlier surveys, amid a debate where immigration emerged as a flashpoint but Suozzi maintained a double-digit edge. High early voting turnout and national Democratic momentum on abortion rights further tilt probabilities, with the February 13 contest poised to flip the seat blue and narrow the House GOP majority.

question: Octra FDV above $100M one day after launch?
description:
This market will resolve to "Yes" if the Fully Diluted Valuation of Octra's governance token is greater than the value specified in the title 1 day after launch. Otherwise, the market will resolve to "No."

The token must be actively, publicly transferable and tradable to be considered a launch.

The FDV will be determined using the total token supply multiplied by the token price.

"1 day after launch" is defined as 4:00 PM ET on the calendar day following launch. The resolution source for this market is the most liquid price source available. If Octra (https://x.com/octra) doesn't launch a token by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, this market will resolve to "No".

question: Will Troy Green be the Democratic nominee for Senate in Oklahoma?
description:
This market will resolve according to the winner of the Democratic Primary for United States Senator from Oklahoma.

If no 2026 Oklahoma Democratic Senate Primary takes place, this market will resolve to "Other".

The resolution source for this market will be the first announcement of the results from the Oklahoma Democratic Party, however an overwhelming consensus of credible reporting may suffice.

events.title: Oklahoma Democratic Senate Primary Winner
events.description:
This market will resolve according to the winner of the Democratic Primary for United States Senator from Oklahoma.

If no 2026 Oklahoma Democratic Senate Primary takes place, this market will resolve to "Other".

The resolution source for this market will be the first announcement of the results from the Oklahoma Democratic Party, however an overwhelming consensus of credible reporting may suffice.

eventMetadata.context_description: N’Kiyla “Jasmine” Thomas leads Oklahoma State Senate District 48 Democratic primary trader consensus at 46% due to her dominant fundraising—over $140,000 raised versus Jim Priest’s $20,000—and endorsements from EMILYs List and Planned Parenthood, bolstering her appeal in urban Oklahoma City. Polls from mid-June show Thomas at 39%, Priest at 18%, Troy Green at 13%, and Rebekah LaVann at 9%, aligning with market pricing reflecting her organizational edge. Priest draws faith-based support as a minister, while Green courts progressives. Early voting started June 13 with low turnout so far, and the June 18 primary looms without major late catalysts shifting odds.

question: Will the Republican Party win the NY-04 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the NY-04 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent polls showing Democratic challenger Laura Gillen leading incumbent Republican Anthony D'Esposito by 5-8 points in this competitive Long Island battleground district form the primary driver of trader consensus favoring Democrats at 76.5% for the NY-04 House seat. Key factors include Gillen's superior fundraising edge, with over $10 million raised compared to D'Esposito's $6 million, bolstering her ad spending in the district Biden narrowly carried in 2020. Recent developments, such as a mid-October Data for Progress survey confirming her lead amid high early voting turnout in Nassau and Suffolk counties, and D'Esposito's vulnerability on issues like abortion rights after his vote against codifying Roe protections, have widened her advantage, reflecting trader assessments of shifting suburban sentiment.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Emerson College polling from October 23-25 shows Democratic nominee Mondaire Jones leading incumbent Republican Mike Lawler 48%-42% in New York's competitive 17th Congressional District, a Hudson Valley battleground that flipped Republican in 2022. This edge aligns with broader aggregates like Race to the WH placing Jones up by 4 points, fueling trader consensus favoring Democrats at 53.5% implied probability amid high early vote turnout and aggressive Democratic ad spending. Lawler's 24.5% standing reflects vulnerability tied to national GOP headwinds and local backlash on issues like property taxes, though the race remains within margin of error ahead of Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Pat Ryan dominates trader sentiment in the NY-18 House race, with markets implying a 90% probability of his victory over Republican challenger Colin DeVries, reflecting the district's shift toward Democratic lean after 2022 redistricting. Ryan's strong polling leads—typically 10-15 points in recent surveys from Emerson and Siena—stem from his proven fundraising edge, over $3 million raised versus DeVries' under $200,000, and endorsements from labor unions and the DCCC. The Hudson Valley district's suburban voters favored Biden by 5 points in 2020, amplifying Ryan's incumbency advantage amid GOP struggles with nominee recruitment and national headwinds. No major shifts recently, though the October 29 debate could introduce volatility.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent polls show Democratic challenger Josh Riley leading incumbent Republican Marc Molinaro by 6-10 points in NY-19, a competitive Hudson Valley district rated Toss-up by forecasters, fueling trader consensus at 57.5% for a Democratic win versus 30.5% Republican. Key drivers include Riley's fundraising edge—over $3 million cash-on-hand—and strong Democratic performance in recent special elections, alongside voter priorities like abortion rights and economic concerns favoring the challenger. Molinaro's incumbency provides a base, but national GOP headwinds and Riley's debate performances have widened the gap since early October aggregates. Early voting data and a final October 28 debate could shift sentiment before November 5.

Separation could emerge from early voting trends starting September 6, debate performances, or super PAC ad surges ahead of November 5.

events.title: Oregon Republican Senate Primary Winner

eventMetadata.context_description: Jo Rae Perkins leads trader consensus for the Oregon Republican U.S. Senate primary at 59.5%, propelled by former President Trump's April endorsement and her dominance in recent polls from DHM Research and others showing a double-digit edge. State Sen. David Brock Smith follows at 31.4%, drawing establishment backing and superior fundraising but trailing amid base preference for Perkins' Trump-aligned conservatism. Lesser contenders like Russell McAlmond (6.2%) and Joe Johnson (4.3%) split remaining support, reflecting fragmented field dynamics. With the May 21 primary approaching, voter turnout among Republicans and final absentee ballot trends could influence outcomes, underscoring current odds as market-implied probabilities.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Chuck Edwards' double-digit polling leads in North Carolina's 11th Congressional District anchor trader consensus at 58% for the GOP, reflecting the seat's strong Republican tilt (Cook PVI R+14) where Donald Trump won by 28 points in 2020. Edwards captured 68% of the vote in 2022 and holds advantages in fundraising and endorsements, with a recent October poll showing him ahead 55-41 against Democrat Nancy Carter. Early voting data indicates robust GOP turnout, bolstering hold odds despite national Democratic enthusiasm potentially energizing the base; traders price in low upset risk absent major shifts before Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Ohio's 1st Congressional District House race, trader consensus slightly favors Democratic incumbent Greg Landsman at 50.5% over Republican challenger Orlando Sonza's 44.0%, reflecting polls like Emerson (Landsman 47%-43%) and Trafalgar (46%-45%) that remain within the margin of error. The contest stays neck-and-neck due to the district's competitive lean—Trump won it by 7 points in 2020—balanced by Landsman's fundraising edge ($3.5M vs. Sonza's $1.2M) and incumbency against GOP national momentum on economy and border issues. Separation could emerge from early voting trends starting late October, a pivotal final debate, or shifts in generic ballot sentiment as Election Day nears.

Separation could emerge from early voting trends starting late October, a pivotal final debate, or shifts in generic ballot sentiment as Election Day nears.

question: Will the Republican Party win the OH-09 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the OH-09 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Emilia Sykes' polling edge and fundraising dominance position the Democratic Party as trader consensus favorite at 58.5% odds to win Ohio's 13th congressional district House seat. A recent October RMG Research poll shows Sykes leading Republican nominee Brian Kazy 51%-42% among likely voters, building on her slim 2022 victory in the battleground district that Biden carried narrowly in 2020. Sykes has raised over $2.6 million versus Kazy's under $200,000, enabling heavier ad spending amid early voting. GOP odds at 16.5% reflect Kazy's primary win but weaker resources and no major momentum shifts, with election day catalysts like turnout poised to influence the tight race.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Democratic Party at 79% in Oregon's 5th Congressional District House race, driven by consistent polling leads for challenger Janelle Bynum over Republican incumbent Lori Chavez-DeRemer. Recent surveys, including a September RMG Research poll showing Bynum ahead 51%-44% and an October Emerson College poll at 48%-43%, reflect her edge in this competitive Central Oregon district, which Trump carried narrowly in 2020. Bynum's fundraising advantage—over $3 million raised versus Chavez-DeRemer's $2.5 million—and endorsements from groups like EMILY's List bolster her position amid a national environment pressuring GOP incumbents in swing areas. Upcoming early voting and the November 5 ballot could shift dynamics, but current evidence sustains Democratic momentum.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the PA-01 House race tilts toward the Republican Party at 50% implied probability, reflecting incumbent Brian Fitzpatrick's consistent polling edge over Democrat Ashley Ehasz in this suburban Philadelphia swing district. Recent Emerson College polling from early October shows Fitzpatrick leading 48%-42%, bolstered by his fundraising advantage—over $3 million raised versus Ehasz's $1.5 million—and cross-party appeal as a moderate with votes against party lines on issues like Ukraine aid. Ehasz has narrowed the gap slightly through aggressive grassroots organizing and local endorsements, but national GOP momentum in competitive House seats sustains the Republican lead amid tight voter turnout expectations ahead of November balloting.

question: Will the Republican Party hold 47 or fewer Senate seats after the 2026 midterm elections?
description:
The 2026 midterm elections are scheduled to be held on November 3, 2026.

This market will resolve according to the number of seats held by the Republican Party in the US Senate as a result of the 2026 midterm elections.

This market will resolve based on the results of all Senate elections, including special elections, that are scheduled to occur in November 2026 as of October 31, 2026. If a required runoff for any such election could change the market’s outcome, the market will remain open until that runoff is conclusively called by this market’s resolution sources.

If a Senate seat is vacant but a corresponding election is not held in November 2026, the seat will be considered held by the party of the seat's most recent incumbent.

A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 Senate elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democratic or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the Senate elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

The resolution source for this market is the Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC. This market will resolve once all three sources have conclusively called all Senate elections. If all three sources do not achieve consensus in calling the relevant races for this market, it will resolve based on the official certification.

Tight dynamics stem from historical midterm losses for the president's party—typically 3-4 seats amid lower turnout—and uncertainty over economic trends, generic ballot trends, and candidate quality in battlegrounds like Maine, North Carolina, Michigan, and Georgia. question: Will the Republican Party hold exactly 49 Senate seats after the 2026 midterm elections?
description:
The 2026 midterm elections are scheduled to be held on November 3, 2026.

This market will resolve according to the number of seats held by the Republican Party in the US Senate as a result of the 2026 midterm elections.

This market will resolve based on the results of all Senate elections, including special elections, that are scheduled to occur in November 2026 as of October 31, 2026. If a required runoff for any such election could change the market’s outcome, the market will remain open until that runoff is conclusively called by this market’s resolution sources.

If a Senate seat is vacant but a corresponding election is not held in November 2026, the seat will be considered held by the party of the seat's most recent incumbent.

A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 Senate elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democratic or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the Senate elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

The resolution source for this market is the Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC. This market will resolve once all three sources have conclusively called all Senate elections. If all three sources do not achieve consensus in calling the relevant races for this market, it will resolve based on the official certification.

question: Will Rio Phillips be the Democratic nominee for Senate in West Virginia?
description:
This market will resolve according to the winner of the Democratic Primary for United States Senator from West Virginia.

If no 2026 West Virginia Democratic Senate Primary takes place, this market will resolve to "Other".

The resolution source for this market will be the first announcement of the results from the West Virginia Democratic Party, however an overwhelming consensus of credible reporting may suffice.

question: Will the Republican Party win the PA-08 House seat?
description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the PA-08 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in Pennsylvania's 8th Congressional District House race strongly favors the Democratic incumbent Matt Cartwright at 76%, reflecting his consistent double-digit polling leads over Republican challenger Rob Bresnahan, including a recent Emerson College survey showing Cartwright ahead 49%-42%. Key drivers include Cartwright's incumbency advantage, superior fundraising with over $1.5 million cash-on-hand versus Bresnahan's under $400,000, and the district's modest Democratic tilt (Biden +5 in 2020). Bresnahan's narrow GOP primary win and Trump endorsement have narrowed the gap slightly from summer polls, but union support and local endorsements bolster Cartwright's position ahead of November's general election.

eventMetadata.context_description: Democratic challenger Janelle Stelson's consistent double-digit leads in recent district polls, including aggregates showing her ahead of incumbent Republican Scott Perry by 10-15 points, anchor trader consensus at 71% for Democrats in Pennsylvania's 10th congressional district House race. Stelson's fundraising dominance—over $5 million raised versus Perry's $1.5 million—bolsters her ad advantage in this R+6 district, while Perry's Freedom Caucus ties and January 6 involvement alienate moderates. A September debate highlighted Stelson's poised performance, shifting odds further despite national GOP momentum. With early voting underway, turnout in suburban and rural areas remains a key uncertainty for the November 5 contest.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Chris Deluzio holds a slight edge in Pennsylvania's 17th congressional district House race, reflected in trader consensus favoring Democrats at 59%, driven by recent Emerson polling showing him ahead 48%-44% over Republican state Rep. Rob Mercuri. Deluzio benefits from strong fundraising exceeding $2 million and DCCC backing in this R+2 swing district spanning Pittsburgh suburbs and Trump-strong Butler County. Mercuri, a self-funded veteran raising over $4.5 million, gains from GOP national momentum but trails in independents. An October 21 debate drew mixed reviews, while early voting trends and final polls could shift tight odds before Election Day.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the TX-09 House race tilts heavily toward the Republican Party at 69.5%, reflecting bets on national GOP momentum amid Donald Trump's strong Texas polling and potential House control scenarios. Incumbent Rep. Al Green (D), a 19-term veteran aged 77 with fundraising superiority ($300K+ cash on hand vs. challenger J. Sean Parnell's modest totals), faces scrutiny over health-related absences—he missed over 80% of votes in the current Congress following 2022 heart surgery. The Houston-based district remains historically Democratic (D+25 partisan lean), but lacks recent polls, leaving traders to weigh GOP gains in urban areas, border security messaging, and early voting starting October 21 against Green's entrenched base.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Republican Monica De La Cruz holds a narrow edge in early polling for Texas's 15th Congressional District, a Hispanic-majority border seat she flipped in 2022, but Democratic challenger Michelle Vallejo's strong fundraising and mobilization efforts among Latino voters have fueled trader consensus tilting slightly toward Democrats at 52%. Recent surveys from firms like internal campaign data and public trackers show the race within 3 points, kept tight by competing narratives on border security, inflation, and economic relief, with turnout among South Texas Hispanics pivotal. National midterm headwinds for the GOP and early vote trends could widen the gap; a debate on October 15 or fresh endorsements might drive separation in this toss-up House contest.

​​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: In Texas's 18th congressional district, a longtime Democratic stronghold in Houston with a D+27 partisan lean, trader consensus heavily favors a Democratic victory at 94.9% implied probability following Rep. Sheila Jackson Lee's death in July and Gov. Greg Abbott's call for a November 5 special election. Harris County Attorney Christian Menefee has surged as the frontrunner after former state Sen. Amanda Edwards exited the race on August 12 and endorsed him, consolidating Democratic support amid polls showing his lead over rivals like ex-Houston Mayor Sylvester Turner. This shift minimizes vote-splitting risks in the plurality-wins format, where past elections saw Democrats exceed 70% of the vote. Realistic challenges include fragmented Democratic turnout from Hurricane Beryl recovery or an unexpectedly unified Republican field, though historical base rates suggest slim odds of a GOP upset.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Rep. Henry Cuellar's strong Democratic primary win (69%-31%) despite a May federal indictment on bribery charges anchors trader consensus for a Democratic victory in Texas's 28th Congressional District. This south Texas seat, with its Democratic-leaning Hispanic-majority electorate (Cook PVI D+17), has backed Cuellar since 2005, including a 20-point 2020 margin, though 2022 was closer at 60%-40%. Republican nominee Tony Villarreal, a former firefighter who won a fragmented GOP primary, faces long odds per forecasters rating it Safe Democratic, with no recent polls or catalysts shifting dynamics ahead of November's general election and early voting.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket tilts toward the Republican candidate at 60.5% implied probability in the TX-32 House race, driven primarily by the district's modest Republican lean—Trump carried it by about 4 points in 2020—and open-seat dynamics following Democratic incumbent Colin Allred's Senate bid. GOP nominee Brandon Herrera, a YouTube personality who upset in the primary, benefits from strong base turnout signals, while Democratic nominee Julie Johnson leads in fundraising but trails in recent polls like a late October survey showing Herrera up 3 points. National GOP momentum in competitive Texas House seats further bolsters trader sentiment, though tight margins and early voting trends keep Democratic chances viable at 41.5%; Election Day on November 5 could shift odds.

eventMetadata.context_description: Incumbent Democrat Vicente Gonzalez maintains a commanding lead in the Texas 34th Congressional District House race, bolstered by recent polls showing him ahead 54%-38% against Republican Ana Cano Lozano, per an October Emerson survey, fueling trader consensus at 68.5% for Democrats. The district's Democratic lean (Biden +18 in 2020) and Gonzalez's fundraising edge—$1.4 million cash-on-hand versus Lozano's $200,000—reinforce this positioning, despite GOP gains among Hispanic voters amid border security debates. Early voting turnout favors Democrats, with no major shifts from the sole post-Labor Day poll, though national headwinds could narrow the gap before November 5.
